coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence probe based on the N, S co-doped dots and silver nanoclusters for the sensitive detection of Hg2+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Pengyuan
Li, Shaoqing
He, Yanzhi
Yao, Guixiang
Yu, Hang
Yang, Wenrong
Wang, Hongbin
Tan, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Exploring highly sensitive fluorescence probe for mercury ion (Hg2+) determination is of great importance for environment system. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence probe using N, S co-doped dots (NS-CDs) and silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) were successful constructed for the determination of Hg2+. In ratiometric fluorescent sensing system, as AgNCs was added to NS-CDs, fluorescence intensity at 442 nm can be reduced but in the increase of intensity at the 762 nm. Inner filter effect (IFE) was attributed to the mainly mechanism of action between the NS-CDs and AgNCs. Interestingly, the fluorescence of NS-CDs/AgNCs at 442 nm was gradually increase after the addition of Hg2+, while fluorescence at 762 nm was gradually quenched. Owing to the formation of NS-CDs/Agn-2NCsHg complexes, the NS-CDs/AgNCs led to a significant conformational change of Hg2+. According to the reverse change of two fluorescence peaks (I765/I442), a novel NS-CDs/AgNCs probe was designed for specific recognition of Hg2+ with the linear range from 0.1 to 90 μM and a limit detection (LOD) of 0.041 μM. Besides, NS-CDs/AgNCs was successful implemented to detect Hg2+ in the sample water with the good recoveries of 92.90–103.64 % (RSD≤4.62 %). Accordingly, the NS-CDs/AgNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe with simplicity, rapidity and high sensitivity exhibited a huge potentiality in the water environment analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S co-doped dots
Silver nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Hg2+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs, AgNCs and NS-CDs/AgNCs. (b) The excitation and emission spectra of the NS-CDs and the Uv-vis spectra of the AgNCs. (c) The fluorescence lifetime of the N,S-CDs and AgNCs.

(a) TEM image of NS-CDs and (b) AgNCs, Inset: the particle size distribution of the NS-CDs and AgNCs by DLS. (c) FT-IR spectra of NS-CDs, AgNCs and NS-CDs/AgNCs. (d) Zeta potential of NS-CDs, AgNCs and NS-CDs/AgNCs.
(a) XPS full spectrum of NS-CDs/AgNCs. The high-resolution of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, (e) S2p and (f) Ag3d spectra.
(a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/AgNCs. (b) UV–vis spectra of NS-CDs, AgNCs and NS-CDs/AgNCs.
The XPS survey spectra of (a) NS-CDs/AgNCs+Hg2+ (b) Ag3d and (c) Hg4f.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs/AgNCs upon different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.1–90 μM). (b) A linear calibration plot between I765/I442 and the concentration of Hg2+. (c) Selectively (orange bar) and anti-interference (blue bar) of NS-CDs/AgNCs in the absence and presence of metal ions and anions.
Process of NS-CDs/AgNCs synthesis and Hg2+ detection.
Comparison of the determination methods for Hg2+.
The results of recovery of Hg2+ determination in real samples.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence probe based on the N, S co-doped dots and silver nanoclusters for the sensitive detection of Hg2+

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring highly sensitive fluorescence probe for mercury ion (Hg2+) determination is of great importance for environment system. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence probe using N, S co-doped dots (NS-CDs) and silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) were successful constructed for the determination of Hg2+. In ratiometric fluorescent sensing system, as AgNCs was added to NS-CDs, fluorescence intensity at 442 nm can be reduced but in the increase of intensity at the 762 nm. Inner filter effect (IFE) was attributed to the mainly mechanism of action between the NS-CDs and AgNCs. Interestingly, the fluorescence of NS-CDs/AgNCs at 442 nm was gradually increase after the addition of Hg2+, while fluorescence at 762 nm was gradually quenched. Owing to the formation of NS-CDs/Agn-2NCsHg complexes, the NS-CDs/AgNCs led to a significant conformational change of Hg2+. According to the reverse change of two fluorescence peaks (I765/I442), a novel NS-CDs/AgNCs probe was designed for specific recognition of Hg2+ with the linear range from 0.1 to 90 μM and a limit detection (LOD) of 0.041 μM. Besides, NS-CDs/AgNCs was successful implemented to detect Hg2+ in the sample water with the good recoveries of 92.90–103.64 % (RSD≤4.62 %). Accordingly, the NS-CDs/AgNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe with simplicity, rapidity and high sensitivity exhibited a huge potentiality in the water environment analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury acts as one of the most toxic metals pollutants in aquatic systems, and its major forms include mercury ion (Hg2+) and methylmercury. Among them, Hg2+ is easily methylated by bacteria in the environment to form methylmercury, which is a highly toxic organic mercury compound (Baimenov et al., 2020; Chaghaghazardi et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2015; Su et al., 2022). When they reach our bodies through the food chain, the human kidney, brain and nervous system are seriously affected, and causes Minamata disease, limb pain, and other diseases (Saleh et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2020). According to the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the maximum contaminant level (MCL) for Hg2+ in drinking water was 10 nM (Song et al., 2023; Veerakumar et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2016). As a serious public health problem, so it has become necessary to detect Hg2+ in the living biological and environment water system (Yin et al., 2023).

The traditional analysis methods to detect Hg2+, for instance, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Kamyabi and Aghaei, 2017), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) (X. Wang et al., 2020), atomic emission spectroscopy (AES) (Li et al., 2014), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Fong et al., 2007), have been reported. Nevertheless, these analysis methods are usually required expensive cost, tedious processing and sophisticated technology (G. Li et al., 2022). In recent years, the fluorescence probe technology with low cost and high efficiency has become increasingly popular for the Hg2+ detection (Y. Li et al., 2022). However, in terms of single fluorescent intensity-based probes, they were more sensitive to some factors, such as photobleaching, pH and the variation of environment polarity, etc. On the contrary, the ratiometric probes with two signals of fluorescence intensity variation could effectively reduce the external disturbance factors via the self-calibrating to improve accuracy (Li et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2023).

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescence nanomaterials, has been extensively used in the detection sensing domain (Wang et al., 2022). CDs attracted a lot of attentions as a potential substrate for fluorescence sensing, because of their several advantages, such as high stability, excellent fluorescent properties and low toxicity. Nevertheless, current CDs for the detection were restricted in their small Stokes shifts and low fluorescent quantum yield (FLQY), which limited their application ability (Khan et al., 2023). Notably, the photoluminescence performance of CDs can be significantly improved by co-doping CDs with some non-metallic elements.

Such as nitrogen and sulfur, they offered more active sites in the original composition to modulate the band gap and add new energy levels, in particular to change the electronic distribution (sp2 and sp3 hybridization) and surface structure of CDs (Romero et al., 2019; Tall et al., 2021; Yue et al., 2022). Therefore, the co-doped CDs not only confer better optical properties and stability, but also the abundant functional groups on the surface also offer potential sites for modifications. In addition, Metal nanoclusters (NCs), as another novel fluorescent nanomaterial, are composed of several to tens metal atoms. Among them, silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) is an important representation in this family (Yang et al., 2023). It was widely used sensing, attributing to their low synthesis cost, wide application, easy functionalization and properties biocompatibility (Yan et al., 2023). Therefore, based on the properties of two different fluorescent nanomaterials, it is necessary to develop a new and simple ratiometric fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection.

In the work, a ratiometric fluorescence probe (NS-CDs/AgNCs) was synthesized for the detection of Hg2+ ( Scheme 1). In brief, citric acid (CA) and glutathione (GSH) were used as raw materials to synthesize NS-CDs by microwave-assisted. AgNCs were prepared using AgNO3 as raw material and L-tyrosine as stabilizer. It was found that when NS-CDs were modified by AgNCs, the fluorescence of NS-CDs at the 442 nm was reduced by the AgNCs, accompanied by fluorescence of AgNCs at the 765 nm was gradually increased. The mainly mechanism of the NS-CDs and AgNCs was attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). By addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the NS-CDs/AgNCs at 442 nm enhanced and decreased at 765 nm. In terms of this property, quantitative detection of Hg2+ was achieved. To sum up, a simple fluorescence strategy for the detection of Hg2+ was established using the fluorescence intensity changes of two different emission centers of NS-CDs/AgNCs.
All reagents and instruments can be found in the supplementary material.

The NS-CDs was fabricated using an approach reported in the literature (Yin et al., 2022). Typically, 1.0 g CA and 1.0 g GSH were dissolved in 20 mL ultra-pure water. Subsequently, the mixture solution was heated by microwave energy at 540 W for 5 min. After the reaction, 20 mL ultra-pure water was dissolved in products, 12,000 rmp for 10 min centrifugation to remove impurity. Then, brown NS-CDs solution was freeze-dried at −40 ◦C for 48 h to obtain powder. The NS-CDs powder was stored in a 4 ◦C refrigerator for further use.

AgNCs were prepared according to the literature (M. Wang et al., 2020). 500 μL AgNO3 (5 mM) was added into the 9 mL L-tyrosine (100 mM). After stirring (450 rmp, 10 min), 500 μL ascorbic acid (50 mM) was mixed and stirred for 5 min at room temperature. The AgNCs crude products were centrifuged (12,000 rmp for 10 min), filtered by microporous membrane (0.22 μm), and purified by a dialysis bag (MWCO: 200–1000) for 48 h. Finally, the AgNCs resulting mixture was stored in refrigerator at 4 ℃.

The NS-CDs/AgNCs as a ratiometric fluorescent probe was self-assembly synthesized as follows: 30 μL of NS-CDs (1 g/L) and 1520 μL of AgNCs were added into 1450 μL of KHP buffer solution (50 mM, pH=4.01). For the determination of Hg2+, the different concentrations of Hg2+ standard solution (0.1–90 μM) was injected into NS-CDs/AgNCs. After incubating for 5 min, the fluorescent signal ratios (I765/I442) of the NS-CDs/AgNCs+ Hg2+ was collected at 442 and 762 nm (Ex=371 nm, 5 nm slit width).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicability and feasibility of NS-CDs/AgNCs, three water samples including river water, lake water and tap water were utilized for assay of Hg2+. Among them, river water and lake water were collected from the Laoyu River and Liangwang Lake of Kunming, China, respectively. Firstly, water samples were filtered by using 0.22 μM microporous membrane. Then, the different concentrations of Hg2+ standard solution (0, 10, 30 and 50 μM) were added into the water sample for further detection.

In this work, the ratiometric fluorescence probe was develop for Hg2+ detection. NS-CDs and AgNCs were employed as two different signal units to synthesize the excellent performance of ratiometric probes. As seen in Fig. 1a, NS-CDs/AgNCs showed two different peaks at 442 nm (I442) and 765 nm (I765). As AgNCs combined with NS-CDs, the fluorescence of NS-CDs at 442 nm was quenched, but the AgNCs enhanced at the 765 nm. Based on this phenomenon, the mechanism between the NS-CDs and AgNCs were explored. In the Fig. 1b, the excitation peak of the NS-CDs was overlapped with the absorption band of the AgNCs. This result may be related to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) (Chen et al., 2022). The fluorescence lifetimes of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/AgNCs were shown in the Fig. 1c. The average lifetime of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/AgNCs were 9.40 and 9.37 ns. In the cases, the lifetimes were basically unchanged. This result shown that IFE was considered the mechanism of action for the NS-CDs/AgNCs probe.

The size and morphology of the NS-CDs and AgNCs were characterized by DLS and TEM, respectively. It can be observed in Fig. 2a that NS-CDs displayed a fine shape and dispersibility in the size with the average of 3.63±1.92 nm(Yin et al., 2023). The AgNCs exhibited spherical shape with average size of 2.19±0.34 nm (Fig. 1b) (Liu and Chen, 2013).

To verify that the NS-CDs/AgNCs was successfully fabricated, its surface functional groups was measured using FT-IR. As displayed in the Fig. 2c, the stretching vibration of -NH and -OH were situated at 3394 cm−1 (Lu et al., 2016). The obvious peak at 2938 cm−1 was belonged to the C-H stretching vibration (Firdaus et al., 2017a). The groups of the C-H, CO and C-N vibration located at the 1710, 1547 and 1397 cm−1, respectively (Zhang et al., 2022). The characteristic band at 1209 and 901 cm−1 were assigned to the C-S stretching vibrations and the para-substituents on the benzene ring. Importantly, compared with NS-CDs/AgNCs, the characteristic stretching vibration of S-H at the 2566 cm−1 and -NH in NS-CDs disappeared and narrowed (Zhao et al., 2019), respectively, suggesting that the S-H and -NH may be converted to Ag-S and Ag-N bond via covalent bonding (Yin et al., 2022).

The surface charge of NS-CDs/AgNCs was measured through zeta potential measurement in Fig. 2d. The zeta potential of NS-CDs/AgNCs showed more positive (-6.34 mV), compared with the NS-CDs (-1.71 mV) and AgNCs (-10.64 mV). This result indicated that NS-CDs and AgNCs attracted each other by electrostatic forces for the formation of NS-CDs/AgNCs.

NS-CDs/AgNCs was measured by the XPS to analyse the chemical compositions and bonds. In the Fig. 3a, the NS-CDs/AgNCs had five peaks at 532.3 eV, 400.5 eV, 366.8 eV, 284.8 eV and 163.08 eV, attributing to O1s, N1s, Ag3d, C1s and S2p, respectively. Three different peaks in the high-resolution C1s spectrum of C-C/CC, C-N and CO located at 284.52 eV, 286.21 eV and 288.32 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b) (Tang et al., 2017). The N1s spectrum can be deconvoluted to three peaks at Ag-N (398.88 eV) and C-N (400.58 eV) as shown in Fig. 3c (Zhang et al., 2022). In Fig. 3d, the O1s spectrum exhibited two peaks at 532.28 eV and 535.88 eV, which represented the CO and C-O (Ren et al., 2017). The XPS spectra of S2p peaks at 163.88 eV, 168.08 eV, and 169.28 eV belonged to the Ag-S, S2p3/2 andS2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 3e) (Maiti et al., 2016). In addition, in the Ag3d spectra (Fig. 3f), The bands at 366.38 eV and 374.38 eV were derived from Ag3d5/2 and Ag3d3/2 (S. Li et al., 2022). The peak of the Ag3d5/2 was divided into Ag (I) (366.27 eV) and Ag (0) (366.38 eV). Ag3d3/2 was attributed to the Ag (I) (374.21 eV) and Ag (0) (374.32 eV). Thus, according to the FTIR and XPS, NS-CDs/AgNCs was successfully prepared by the NS-CDs and AgNCs.

To enable the ratiometric fluorescence quantification of Hg2+, the fluorescence system needs to include two signals of NS-CDs and AgNCs. For this purpose, the optical properties were investigated using by UV–vis absorption and FL spectra. In the Fig. 4a, the NS-CDs and AgNCs emission peaks at 445 nm, and 442 and 765 nm under excitation wavelengths at 371 and 379 nm, respectively. The UV−vis spectrum of NS-CDs shown a distinct absorption peak at 348 nm (Fig. 4b), owing to the n-π* transition of the surface state containing C-O and C-N bonds. The absorption spectrum of AgNCs did not appear surface plasmon resonance peak in the range at 380–420 nm, which manifested that the AgNCs did not occur large aggregation. The NS-CDs/AgNCs displayed an obvious absorption peak at 274 nm accompanied by the disappearance of the absorption peak of NS-CDs, suggesting may be the formation of a new compounds.

In order to obtain higher sensitivity and stable detection, the experimental conditions were optimized at various quality ratio, pH and reaction temperatures. As shown in the Fig. S1a, the optimum quality ratio of the NS-CDs and AgNCs were recorded in 1:10. In the Fig. S1b, NS-CDs/AgNCs shown a good stability when the pH from 2 to 12. pH 7 was chosen as the optimum condition for the subsequent detection. Similarly, NS-CDs/AgNCs exhibited a high stability from 20 to 60 ℃ (Fig. S1c), the optimum temperature was selected at 25 ℃. Besides, ion strength is vital for the detection in solution. The fluorescence signals almost unaffected at NaCl concentration of 0–1 M (Fig. S1d)., showing an excellent resistance. Besides, as shown the Table S1, compared with other methods for optimum detection condition, NS-CDs/AgNCs indicated a satisfactory stability.

The mechanism of fluorescence response of the NS-CDs/AgNCs was investigated toward Hg2+. In the presence of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs/AgNCs increased at 442 nm, while the fluorescence signal at 765 nm decreased. The phenomenon may be attributed to a redox reaction between the NS-CDs and AgNCs (Yin et al., 2022; Saenchoopa et al., 2021; Liu and Huang, 2013; Zhou et al., 2019). This reaction equation was described as follow:
NS-CDs/AgNCs + Hg2+ → NS-CDs/Agn-2NCsHg + 2Ag+

The redox reaction can occur normally due to the standard potential of Hg2+/Hg (0.85 V) was higher than the Ag+/Ag (0.80 V) (Firdaus et al., 2017b). To further prove this reaction process, the chemical state of Ag in the NS-CDs/AgNCs+Hg2+ was determined by XPS analysis, the high-resolution spectra of Ag was displayed in Fig. 5e. Compared with the Ag3d spectra of the NS-CDs/AgNCs, in the presence of Hg2+, more Ag(I) was converted from Ag(0) in AgNCs. In addition, the Hg4f spectra also confirmed this conclusion. As shown in Fig. 4f, newly appeared signal of Hg0, validating the proposed redox reaction in equation. Thus, the reason why the fluorescence was released at 442 nm by the Hg2+ can be attribute to the redox reaction between AgNCs and Hg2+.

Under the optimal conditions, a ratiometric fluorescence strategy was developed for detecting Hg2+. In the Fig. 6a, the NS-CDs/AgNCs at the 442 nm was gradually increased, while the fluorescence signal at 765 nm declined with the increase of Hg2+ concentrations (0.1–90 μM). In Fig. 6b, a good linear correlation (I 765/I 442=-0.0003274×C Hg 2+ + 0.1031, R
2 =0.9922) was obtained with an excellent linear range (0.1–90 μM). The LOD was obtained to be 0.041 μM based on the 3 N/S (S is the slope of the calibration line, N is the standard deviation of blank samples). Besides, as shown the Table 1, Compared with other methods for Hg2+ detection, NS-CDs/AgNCs indicated a satisfactory detection range and LOD.

The selectively and anti-interference serve as an important parameter to evaluate the fluorescence probe. The different metal ions and anions including K+, Al3+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Co3+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, I-, Br-, Cl-, HCO3 -, PO4 2- and Hg2+ were measured to investigate the selectivity of the NS-CDs/AgNCs. As displayed in the Fig. 6c, the fluorescence ratio signals (I765/I442) of the NS-CDs/AgNCs was only increase by the Hg2+, compared with the other ions and anions. In the meantime, the anti-interference of the NS-CDs/AgNCs was done. The NS-CDs/AgNCs had a good anti-interference capability when the 3-fold of Hg2+ was added. Based on the above results, NS-CDs/AgNCs displayed a high selectivity and anti-interference, which shown a huge potential in the Hg2+ detection.

To evaluate the feasibility of this analytical method, some real sample was evaluated by the NS-CDs/AgNCs for Hg2+. As summarized in Table 2, various concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 10, 30, 50 μM) were mixed into the samples, and then measured the fluorescence signals. The mean recovery was between 92.90 % and 103.64 %, and RSD ≤ 4.62 %. This result indicated that NS-CDs/AgNCs has better accuracy and higher reliability.

In conclusion, a ratiometric probe (NS-CDs/AgNCs) was successfully developed for the detection of Hg2+ based on the NS-CDs with AgNCs. NS-CDs covalently bonded to AgNCs, showing satisfactory stability. Particularly, after the addition of Hg2+, the NS-CDs/AgNCs generated double detection and reverse fluorescence signals to improve its sensitivity. The detection mechanism of the Hg2+ attributed to the redox reaction, which resulted in the formation of NS-CDs/Agn-2NCsHg complexes. Upon addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence of AgNCs declined while that of NS-CDs increased in probe system. Based on the intensity ratio (I765/ I442), a novel detection strategy was established for Hg2+ detection. The linear range of NS-CDs/ AgNCs+Hg2+ was 0.1–90 μM (R2=0.9922) with a limit detection (LOD) 0.041 μM. The satisfactory recovery in real water samples were gained. These satisfying results proved that the ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibited a good ability of detection and a huge potential for evaluating trace Hg2+ in water environment.

Wei Tan: Writing – review & editing. Hongbin Wang: Writing – review & editing. Wenrong Yang: Investigation. Hang Yu: Software. Guixiang Yao: Conceptualization. Yanzhi He: Methodology. Shaoqing Li: Data curation. Pengyuan Yin: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22266036), the Yunnan Provincial Department of Science and Technology (No. 202105AC160055), Functional Nanomaterial-based Chemical and Biological Sensing Technology Innovation Team of Department of Education of Yunnan Province, and the College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.psep.2024.06.073.
